speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 5, 1995. The survivor is Dennis Urstein, D-E-N-N-I-S U-R-S-T-E-I-N. I'm Paula Draper, P-A-U-L-A D-R-AP-E-R. We're in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, conducting an interview in English. It is a continuation of an interview begun on July 21, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, my name is Dennis Urstein. And I'm restarting my story of the day of liberation. We're liberated the 29th of April, 1945. We're free. What's freedom? You don't know how to handle it. I don't know how handle it. I don't know what freedom is. Is it uh, something that uh, there are no beatings, no killings? We have food all of a sudden. We're being sprayed with DDT on ever corner that we are taking from the barracks. The American soldiers that liberated us are beautiful people. And again, we are-- we are stunned. We are uh, almost anesthetized, so we don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: We wander around. We're looking. What are we looking for? We don't know what we're looking for. But, all of a sudden-- uh, uh, I learned English in-- in-- in-- school, in 1936. And all of a sudden, this young fella comes up. I think he was a sergeant. I'm not sure. And he starts to talk to me in pigeon German, pigeon English-German. And I said to him, I understand you. He said, really? Anyhow, the friendship with this young man started right down there. I-- I assume he would be about ah, 22 or 23, or around that, OK? So uh, we made an appointment to see each next day. So he comes next day, and he brings me a big satchel full of chocolate, Hershey chocolate. I ate so much Hershey chocolate that after two days, I couldn't look at another chocolate. So I gave it to my friends in the barracks. I used to be known as the chocolate man. And we talked, and we we talked, and we talked, and uh, his attitude was such that he was-- he was horrified. He was horrified what he saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me that he saw a train load-- maybe 10 of 12 cars-- of people that they discovered as they marched in. And most of the people in the cars were dead. So when they opened it up, I mean, uh, you know I-- the impact of how he thought came to me much later. Because I-- I-- if I can use that word, he went bananas. You know, he really flipped. He couldn't understand. I mean, this man went through the whole campaign, from-- from-- from D-Day to-- to-- to the liberation of Dachau, and then carried on, I am sure. And he just couldn't believe what he saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, you know, for many years, I have tried to uh-- yea, he let-- he gave me a photograph. And he gave me a name. He called me-- what did he call me? Johnny. I guess everybody was a Johnny. And he signed it, uh, uh, best of luck. Ah, no-- to Johnny, best of luck, Paul. I wish I could see this man. I would like to kiss his nose. But uh, you know. I tried to-- I-- I-- I send his picture that I have here to uh, different stations in the United States. But I was turned down. Because it's such a long shot, you know? But what a beautiful person he was. What do you mean stations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, the CBC-- ah, the CBS. And I got a letter from one of the uh-- I have it here somewhere, the-- no, but-- and they said that they appreciate. And but they just can't do anything. They send me back the picture. It was a copy anyhow. I have the original here. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you sent the picture out so that maybe they would do a story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told them the story. That's right, I told them the story. But nothing was done. And I-- you know, I don't know where he comes from. I haven't got the faintest idea. Even if I-- if he would have told me, I probably would have forgotten. Because you know, it's a heck of a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, the other incident is that we heard, on the second or third day, that there were some SS men that changed into our uniforms-- our dress uniforms-- and somehow uh-- uh, ming-- mingled in the crowd down there. And we found one. He made a very, very big error. He had the-- he had the striped uniform. But instead of-- and-- and-- and-- he shaved his head, very short. It was just shorter than a crew cut-- something like that, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: But what he did was, he wore the wrong cap. He wore the cap of a [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH] wore green hats in Dachau. It's a peak cap. I have it here. So I can show it to you later, if I may pick it up, and uh-- this is the cap that this guy wore. No. The word Dachau was not on there. It was not on. I put this on later. I found uh, a dress uniform in one of the barracks down there.  I cut it out, and put that on there as a souvenir. And what I kept in this uh, cap was soap. I was crazy about soap. I felt so unclean that I-- that I-- that I was really crazy about it. Now this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did-- how did you come across the hat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this man uh, was caught by-- ah, or was-- was recognized by three prisoners down there. And to make it uh, very short and very uh-- we got him behind the crematorium, and we hung him. There were four-- five of us. That's right. Now, these five people that, uh, did that-- we kept in touch for a very, very long time. And uh, I'm the only one left. The rest died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- what was going on in your head when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was very satisfied. I was happy. I-- I-- you see, there is one thing that you must understand. As soon as I-- as one says, I caught-- I caught my breath, I had one thing on my mind, vengeance. I-- it-- I can taste it right now. We-- we wanted to kill. This is really what we wanted to do. We didn't want to uh, bring him to justice by taking him to the Americans, or taking him anywhere. Ah, OK, then the next big occasion on Dachau was that the commander of the armed forces down there decided to bring the whole village in there. And this is another incident that sticks in my mind. Um, the whole village came in. There were old men. There were young kids. There were women, and everybody. And when they saw all the bodies that they took 'em to the crematorium down there-- because the crematoria couldn't cope with all the bodies that were there. So they looked at them. And I-- speaking the language-- since I'm from Vienna, Bavarian is-- is almost the same dialect.

speaker: interviewer
text: I heard these things-- oh, my God. We didn't know what happened. How could anything like this happen? And I got so angry. And I said to one guy-- I  said-- and I used some very bad language. I said, how the hell can you say a thing like this? You lived here all your life and you saw all these people coming in. You traded with the [NON-ENGLISH], with the people down there. They needed food. They needed provisions. You-- they bought it from you guys down there. And you don't know what's going on down there? Well, uh, [SIGHS] I had one desire. I wanted to go back to Belgium. And the reason I wanted to go back to Belgium is that uh, this was the last place that my parents lived. And I knew where they lived. I knew the address. I even know the address today. It was [NON-ENGLISH] number nine. And uh, since I spoke Flemish, I uh-- the interrogator down there, or the repatriation officer down there, ask everybody, where do you come from? And I said, from Belgium. So I got into a Belgium transport. Ah, the people that-- that went back with me knew that I was not Belgian. Because my-- my language wasn't that good. But we were comrades. I mean, what the heck? They didn't care why I wanted to go back oh-- yeah. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19 and 45. Uh, we left, I think, in uh, April-- beginning of-- beginning of-- uh, no. In the middle of May. Somewhere around that time. I don't know the exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date of your liberation again?

speaker: interviewee
text: 29 Of April, 1945. So it was about few weeks later. I was very skinny. And I-- I was-- I looked horrible. But my hair started to grow, funny enough. I think, uh-- I wish it would grow right now. But, uh-- uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you've mentioned we, and the people you went with, and people that you participated in this hanging with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they didn't go with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people that were your friends for any period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, I knew them for quite some time-- for about, oh, couple of years. They were all from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd all been together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And ah--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you maintain contact--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this early period as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until they died. No, we started-- uh, we having contact, I think, in 1900 and 47 or 48, then. Because then I saw them in-- in-- in displaced person, and DP camps, you know. And uh, we took our friendship up. And they all went different directions. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So right in this immediate post-war period, everyone went, including yourself, on their own personal missions? That's right. I guess so. Well, there-- it was different. You see, again-- and I'm not-- I don't knock him. And many people had different ideas and acted different. I wanted vengeance. That was my main object. OK, there were girls. And there was food, obviously. That-- but it didn't come in that order, you know. But this is what I really wanted. So I went back to Belgium. And I arrive at the uh-- at the railway station. And all I had was my striped tunic and uh, my pants. And I had a pair of old shoes and that cap on. And um, all of a sudden, this young man-- maybe in his middle 20s, a little shortstop as I'd call him-- comes up, and says to me in Flemish, are you Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yes. So he-- he whips out ah-- ah pad. And he writes down something. And he says, OK. This is where you're going and eat. This is where you're going to go and get your clothes. This is where you're going to sleep tonight. And then he hands me 50 bucks. You know, when that happened-- huh, still hits me today. It was the most beautiful moment of my life after war. Because I said to myself, hey, I'm back in the family. Anyhow, I stayed in Belgium and ah-- for, I think, about three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was this a Jewish social agency?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was.  It was the-- what they-- what they would call here the Jewish Congress, or something like that. I do not know the proper name. But it was the-- there was only one Jewish organization at that time, anyhow. So we went there. And ah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same place where my parents lived. The landlady let me in. But there was absolutely nothing down there. She told me that uh, my mother was picked up on the street. And my father, that looked like an Aryan, hearing about it, volunteered, and joined her. And that was it. Anyhow. We uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you own the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was rented. It was  a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would-- why would she let you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because she was-- she-- when I told her who I was, she started to cry. And she told me that she probably thinks that my parents are not alive anymore. And she was a wonderful lady. I went to the ah-- ah, American headquarters in Brussels. That was in Brussels, OK? And ah, I uh, introduced myself, told them who I was, showed them my number. That's the only thing we had. Although we received identity cards immediately from the Belgian government. It said, [NON-ENGLISH], which means foreigner, you know. And it-- in it with my picture on there. And it-- it was horrible. But-- so I introduced myself down there. And I said, if I can be of service.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to do? I said, I want to catch these guys that did these things. So they send me to uh-- into a room down there. And there was a man that interviewed me. And then he told me that he was with the uh, CIC, which is the Counter Intelligence Corp. And he said, uh, we will send you to Vienna, because that is you home town.  I said, fine. So I picked up and uh, I went on the train. Well, what kind of qualifications did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The qualifications-- the language. I spoke perfect uh, German and Viennese, you know, the dialects. And I spoke English. I spoke Dutch, which incidentally I've forgotten completely. Dutch and Flemish is the same thing. It's just a different dialect. The pronunciation is different. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what exactly did they recruit you for?

speaker: interviewee
text: To apprehend war criminals. Very simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why would they accept you for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a lot people that were in the camps uh, that did that. And there-- as a matter of fact, in the department itself, which I found out later on, there were a lot of people that were Yugoslavs, that were partisans. And they had them too. So you know, they had a whole-- a real mixed bag down there. But we all got along very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what evidence did they require from you about your background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well they-- if-- once we-- once-- well, there were two ways of getting in. A, if somebody reported seeing somebody, we received word from the commanding officer, whoever was in charge, to tell us that this was the guy. And if they had a picture in SS uniform, then they gave a picture. And then  we started looking for him. And we usually found him. We didn't bring him back. But we found him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Salzburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wait-- ah, 21. Ah, the only thing is that-- uh, uh, this is very important. You see, as soon as I crossed the border from Germany, in a place called Freilassing, which is-- this is on one side. In Germany there's the border town. And the next town is Salzburg. And I heard the conductor speak. I made up my mind about something. Because it really-- I said, if I go back to Vienna, I have a lot of scores to settle. And the next thing I think-- I'm going to get killed if I going to go down there. Because I'm going to kill them. This was my attitude. I was immature. But at the same time, I grew under circumstances that hardened you. And uh, anyway, I got off in Salzburg. So I reported to ah, army headquarter. And there was ah-- ah, a captain down there who uh, was uh, from New York-- I think he was from the Bronx-- by the name of Goldstein. And I told him my story. And I said look, if I go back to Salzburg, I'm going to do things that are not going to be good for me, and certainly not going to be good for you, whatever it is. In Salzburg, you have the four forces. You have the English, the Russians, the French, and the Americans. [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. And uh-- You mean Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna. I'm sorry, yes. So I stayed in Salzburg. And uh, I did my work. I had a cover down there. They uh, found a detective agency, a civil detective agency. And uh, for all intents and purposes, I worked for him, for the detective agency, for the owner of the detective agency.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Schachinger, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-- S-C-H-A-C-H-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the name of the owner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the owner of the detective agency, that's right. Then I forgot his first name.

words.text: Then I forgot his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where it was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. Well, it was near-- near the river down there, near the Salzach, in the center of, more or less of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say you were-- you had scores to settle in Vienna, these are the people you grew up with? Is that who you were thinking of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, especially two-- two especially. And we'll come to them a little later. Um, so I worked down there. And uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were working with people from all kinds of backgrounds that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all-- all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- oh no, not all of them. A lot of them were, but not all of them. No, no, I would say that the percentage was maybe 80-20. In other words 20% were Jewish and 80% were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Americans were involved in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, maybe about three, four dozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their background?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Army-- Army personnel that just happened to uh, work for the CIC. And there-- there was another section down there, which was the CID, the Criminal Investigation Department. Um, so we worked. And we found them. And we didn't bring them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember that one day, uh, Captain Goldstein said to me-- he says, I don't understand you guys. Meaning by guys, he mean the four of us. We were all-- these four, that did that in Dachau, were the ones that worked with me. And uh, he said, I knew that you got them, but where are they? I said, well, they are somewhere. And that's what we did. What were the names of the people you worked with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to tell you. That's one thing I don't want to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what-- uh, give me an example?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not-- they're not alive, anyhow. So what's the difference? But they're not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an example of what would happen when you found someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we executed them. It's very simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, can can you go through an example of  what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't want to. We executed them. They're not around anymore. And they deserved-- they didn't deserve to-- to be in court. They didn't deserve to-- to live. And they deserved to die. And that was our attitude. It changed later on. But at that time, as I say, my mind was made up about one thing and-- vengeance. It was the biggest thing I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they living with their families, most of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not all of them. We-- I think we caught about 11, yeah, that I know. And then later on, it was different. Because we had to bring them in. And there were certain things that happened. Uh, you see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the 11 that you executed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all German and Austrian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Austrians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what would have been their activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them were guards. Uh, I think three of them were in a place called Mauthausen. You know, Salzburg and Salzkammergut was the hiding place for all the Nazis down there. That's where they all went underground. I mean, most of them were caught down there. Even the bigwigs that were in Nuremberg were-- were all caught in that area down there. And uh, so uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we told them, after we caught them. And then you heard that usual story about I was forced to do what-- what? We are forced to kill children? We are forced to beat somebody to death? Who forced you? We didn't see anybody behind you with a gun to force you. And then the most, uh-- I would say that the thing that bothered me more than anything else was the story that I always heard-- I was a soldier. [PAUSE FOR 3 SECONDS] And we said, you're a soldier? You're a murderer, not a soldier. But anyhow, ah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any doubts about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We never had any doubt. Because the first thing that we asked them to-- the first thing-- was to take off their shirt and lift their arm. If they lifted their arm, and we saw their blood group down there, we knew that we didn't make a mistake. And besides it, they didn't change much. They-- they were maybe a little slimmer than we saw them. But that was it. These are unhappy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, you said later then, you began to bring them in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. But what happened to them was a long story that-- that you see, the American soldiers that was in the front line, that went through for the whole campaign, was a different sort of fella than these so-called civil administration that came afterwards, OK? When they send these guys home, that fought through the whole campaign, the new ones came in. And they saw a beautiful city, especially in Salzburg. They saw beautiful girls. And if the beautiful girls' fathers were bigwigs down there, nothing happened to them. You see, the-- it was just like the unbelievable. They were not revisionists. But they didn't know. They were young, 21- and 22-year-olds that came in the second wave of soldiers that came to Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Enough of that for right now. Let's talk about something very, very nice, OK? I met my wife. I think this a-- this is the best story. As a matter of fact, in about 9 days we're going to be married 49 years. And she needs-- she-- she deserves six medals, not just one. Because I'm not the easiest guy to live with. And the reason is that I always talk about my past. And I-- she's not sick and tired of it. But she says, look-- and I can't. I-- this is-- this is the problem, you see. I-- I keep on thinking about it. Something will start me off on-- I don't know, if it's-- if she says, oh, the bread is old. We're going to throw it out. That's all I have to hear. You understand? Small things, or um, I have enough. I don't want to eat anymore. What do you mean, you don't want to eat anymore? Do you know what it-- what it would meant to us you would have had those six bites left? These are the things. And I give her credit for it. And I love her very much. Have you always been like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: All though my years, yes. I would say so. That was my-- the baggage that I carry around. And that's the unfortunate part. You know, I've-- I think I've said it before, but I going to mean-- I say it very quickly again, there are people that I know that were in camp that can't forget. But they can live with it.  In another words, they don't want to talk about it. Uh, sometimes when we get together we talk about incidents, or something like this. But I live with this bloody thing form the moment I wake up until I go to bed. There's always something that turns me to think about events that happened at that time. But let's talk about my wife. She was the most beautiful-- oh, was she gorgeous. She still is. She's beautiful. But she was-- she was-- she was my lifesaver. She uh-- she calmed me down. And uh, we think that uh-- OK, so a year later, we had uh, our daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Claire. Claire. C-L-A-I-R-E. Claire de Lune.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, what do you mean, her maiden name? That's her-- that's her name, Claire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you mean her maiden name. Uh, Kimmerling. K-I-M-M-E-R-L-I-N-G. I think it's two M's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, uh, I met her at the street car stop. There was a guy down there-- oh, this is funny. It started to rain. I had a Jeep and a driver. And the driver was a Dutchman. And uh, all of a sudden, it stopped. It didn't work. The machine didn't go. So I got off. And uh, it was opposite the theater. There's a theater down there called [NON-ENGLISH] in Salzburg. And there was a street car stop. And uh, so uh, I stood there. And it was raining. And all of a sudden, a guy comes up to me. And he says to me, hey, Dolfy. That's my real name, Dolfy. I said, how are ya? I haven't seen you for a long time-- da, da, da da. I knew him from the camp. And he said, do you know what? It's my birthday tonight. And I hired a nightclub. Why don't you come?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he turns around. And he walks over to a woman, to a girl, and he talks to her. And he brings her over, introduces me. That was Claire. He said, you know, you guys live in the same area. Why don't you come together? So we did. And uh, I was told uh, after-- after we got engaged, that she hated my guts the first night, because I kept on singing in her ear when I was dancing. I had a very-- I had a good voice, really. And uh, so we were very happy. And we moved in together. And we got married I think three months after we met. We were engaged four or five-- four weeks after that. And-- um-- And what is Claire's background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Claire was in the labor camp from 1900 and [PAUSE] '40, I think, until '41 or '42. And then she was sent back to Vienna and told to report in a certain place. And she didn't. And it was her luck. Because then she went underground. She was shoveled from one place to the other. They used to call them submarines. And she lived through the whole thing. And then, when the war was over-- uh that-- because that was the first question I asked her. I said, why are you here? I didn't know she was Jewish. Why are you hear? You-- you don't talk like a woman from Salzburg. She says, no, I'm from Vienna. Well, she told me, when the Russians came in, they used to rape everybody down then. And she got scared. And she got away. And uh, that's when I knew. And then she told me about her father, that her father was Buchenwald and got killed down there, either in Buchenwald or in Auschwitz. He was arrested, I think, in 1939. Her mother went to England. Her sister went to England as domestics, but she was left with her father. And uh, that's-- that's the, uh-- the ninth wonder of the world, when I met my wife. She's beautiful. Oh, she gives me a lot of hell. But she's beautiful and I love her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she talk about her experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. She-- uh, she has forgotten-- just the other day, we were sitting down.  We were talking about it. And I tried to get something out of her. She does remember, and she doesn't remember. Because she-- she is very, very lucky that she get rid of her past. And uh, so that's-- we uh-- oh, I forgot something. But I'm going to tell you later. Because I think that I haven't got time now. But as I said, my sister was in England. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me just ask you about your children. Where were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, my-- my children-- one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: One daughter. In Salzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marilyn. Well, actually her name is Marie, M-A-R-I-E, after my mother. But then I had an uncle, in Switzerland. And he called her Marylee. And so from Marylee, it became Marilyn. And she doesn't-- she doesn't want to go by the name of Marie. On her passport, it says Marie, obviously-- Marie Marilyn. But it's Marie. That was my mother's name.
